hook: An antiquarian shop of uncommon books and print-age ephemera, home to the world's first randomizing vintage-book vending machine.
facts: Antiquarian bookshop founded in 2006 by Stephen Fowler, specializing in uncommon books and paper artifacts from the age of print.
Originally on Dundas West near Dovercourt; relocated in spring 2016 to 1067 Bloor St W in Bloordale Village, where it remains open.
Houses the Biblio-Mat, billed as the world's first randomizing vending machine for old books, which dispenses a random vintage book for a few dollars.
The Biblio-Mat was designed and built by Craig Small (of animation studio The Juggernaut) and introduced in 2012.
Listed by the International League of Antiquarian Booksellers (ILAB) as an affiliate shop.
w: An antiquarian shop of uncommon books and print-age ephemera in Bloordale -- and home to the Biblio-Mat, billed as the world's first randomizing book vending machine. Feed it a few coins and it clunks out a random vintage book, no choosing allowed. Stephen Fowler founded the shop in 2006; the machine arrived in 2012.

a: University of Toronto, St. George campus
hook: Canada's largest publicly accessible repository of rare books and manuscripts, holding everything from 11th-century manuscripts to Canada's only Shakespeare First Folio.
facts: Located at 120 St. George Street on the University of Toronto's St. George campus, immediately south of Robarts Library.
Constitutes the largest repository of publicly accessible rare books and manuscripts in Canada, holding roughly 700,000 volumes and about 3,500 linear metres of manuscripts.
Holdings include manuscripts dating back to the 11th century and Canada's only copy of the 1623 Shakespeare First Folio.
Holds the manuscript papers of Margaret Atwood and Leonard Cohen, among other authors.
Open to all scholars and researchers regardless of University of Toronto affiliation; onsite registration is required but a U of T library card is not.
w: Canada's largest open repository of rare books and manuscripts, behind a fortress of concrete at U of T. Some 700,000 volumes run from 11th-century manuscripts to the country's only 1623 Shakespeare First Folio, alongside the papers of Atwood and Leonard Cohen. Any researcher may register and handle them, U of T card or not.

hook: A hidden Sherlock Holmes sanctum atop the Toronto Reference Library, decorated like 221B Baker Street and holding one of the world's foremost Conan Doyle collections.
facts: Housed on the 5th floor of the Toronto Reference Library at 789 Yonge Street, within the Marilyn & Charles Baillie Special Collections Centre (renovated 2014).
Holds one of the world's largest research collections devoted to Arthur Conan Doyle and Sherlock Holmes, with 25,000+ items.
The room is decorated to evoke Holmes's 221B Baker Street apartment, with Victorian-era furniture, a Persian carpet and floor-to-ceiling wooden shelves.
Collection includes a first edition of The Hound of the Baskervilles and original Sherlockian illustrations by Sidney Paget and Frederic Dorr Steele.
The room is open to the public, who may browse the materials freely.
w: A hidden Sherlock Holmes sanctum on the fifth floor of the Toronto Reference Library, dressed like 221B Baker Street -- Victorian furniture, a Persian carpet, floor-to-ceiling shelves. It guards one of the world's great Conan Doyle collections, 25,000 items deep, with a first edition of The Hound of the Baskervilles.

n: Merril Collection of Science Fiction, Speculation & Fantasy
a: College Street / Lillian H. Smith Branch
hook: A specialist non-circulating library of over 80,000 speculative-fiction works founded around author Judith Merril's personal collection.
facts: Located on the 3rd floor of the Toronto Public Library's Lillian H. Smith Branch at 239 College Street.
Contains over 80,000 items spanning science fiction, fantasy, horror and magic realism.
Grew out of science-fiction author Judith Merril's personal collection, donated to the library in the 1970s (originally the Spaced Out Library).
Materials are non-circulating and may only be consulted in the collection's reading room.
Holds original science-fiction and fantasy cover art alongside books, magazines and pulps.
w: A non-circulating library of over 80,000 works of science fiction, fantasy, horror and the speculative, grown from the personal hoard of the writer Judith Merril. She donated it in the 1970s -- it began life, gloriously, as the Spaced Out Library. Pulps, first editions and original cover art, read only in its reading room.

hook: A museum devoted entirely to footwear and the study of shoes, in a Raymond Moriyama building shaped like a shoebox with the lid askew.
facts: Located at 327 Bloor Street West, at the southwest corner of St. George and Bloor, near the northwest of U of T's St. George campus.
A museum of footwear and calceology (the study of footwear), founded on the collection of Sonja Bata.
Housed in a three-storey Deconstructivist building designed by Moriyama & Teshima Architects, with Raymond Moriyama as lead, evoking a shoebox.
Collection spans roughly 4,500 years of footwear history from around the world.
w: A museum devoted entirely to footwear, in a Raymond Moriyama building shaped like a shoebox with the lid set askew. Sonja Bata's collection spans some 4,500 years of shoes from around the world. Calceology -- the study of footwear -- has rarely had a grander home. Everything from Egyptian sandals to celebrity heels, under one tilted lid.

n: SkullStore Oddity Shop & Prehistoria Natural History Centre
hook: An oddity emporium and natural-history centre crammed with real skulls, taxidermy, fossils and some 30,000 macabre artifacts.
facts: Now located at 333 Yonge Street, Unit 2, near Yonge-Dundas Square (the entrance is upstairs, not at street level).
Combines a retail oddity shop (SkullStore) selling real skulls, taxidermy, fossils and curios with the Prehistoria Natural History Centre museum.
First opened a brick-and-mortar storefront in 2015 on Weston Road; relocated downtown to Yonge Street after an eviction tied to redevelopment.
The combined space is about 21,000 square feet and holds roughly 30,000 artifacts.
Open seven days a week, noon to 8 p.m.
w: An oddity emporium and natural-history centre stacked with real skulls, taxidermy, fossils and some 30,000 macabre curios. Part shop, part museum -- you can buy a skull or just gawp at the bones. It started on Weston Road in 2015, was evicted, and now hides upstairs off Yonge-Dundas Square. Twenty-one thousand square feet of the strange.

hook: A free Victorian glasshouse complex whose 1910 cast-iron-and-glass Palm House anchors a year-round indoor botanical garden downtown.
facts: Located at 19 Horticultural Avenue, within Allan Gardens park (bounded by Carlton, Sherbourne, Gerrard and Jarvis Streets).
The centrepiece Palm House, a domed Victorian glasshouse, was designed by architect Robert McCallum and completed in 1910.
The conservatory complex houses themed greenhouses with palms, tropical and arid plants and seasonal displays, and is free to visit.
The Palm House reopened on May 14, 2025 after a three-year, $11.3-million heritage restoration led by HRI Group with Zeidler Architecture.
The restoration replaced window glazing and the overhead glazing of all three domes with fritted glass and modernized ventilation and climate controls.
w: A free Victorian glasshouse anchored by a domed cast-iron Palm House of 1910, holding a year-round tropics in the middle of downtown. Robert McCallum designed the dome; palms, orchids and desert plants fill the themed wings around it. A restoration reopened the Palm House in 2025. Greenhouse warmth, on the coldest Toronto day.

hook: A fully built second subway platform beneath Bay Station that carried passengers for just six months in 1966 before being sealed off.
facts: Lower Bay is the disused lower level of Bay Station at 1240 Bay Street, opened in 1966 as part of an experimental 'interlining' scheme connecting the Bloor-Danforth and Yonge-University lines.
The interlining experiment was abandoned after about six months because delays on one line cascaded across the whole system.
The platform and tracks still exist and are used by the TTC to train operators, move trains between lines, and for subway filming without disrupting service.
It has appeared in films including The Taking of Pelham One Two Three, Johnny Mnemonic and Mimic.
It is occasionally opened to the public for events such as Doors Open Toronto and Nuit Blanche.
legend: Local lore holds the disused platform is haunted by a 'Lady in Red' said to appear to TTC workers wearing a red dress; some crews are said to be uneasy working the tunnels there. This is folklore, not documented fact.
w: A fully built second subway platform beneath Bay Station that carried passengers for just six months in 1966, then was sealed off. The 'interlining' experiment -- weaving two lines together -- collapsed when one line's delays cascaded across the whole system. The TTC still uses the ghost platform to train drivers and shoot films.

hook: The upper hall of the Elgin and Winter Garden, the last surviving Edwardian double-decker (stacked) theatre in the world, shuttered for sixty years before its restoration.
facts: The Elgin and Winter Garden Theatres at 189 Yonge Street are a pair of stacked theatres built in 1913 as the flagship of Marcus Loew's chain, designed by architect Thomas W. Lamb.
The upper-level Winter Garden opened on 16 February 1914 as an 'atmospheric' theatre styled as a garden under the stars, with murals, trellises and lantern lights.
The Winter Garden closed in 1928 when sound film took over the lower theatre, and remained shuttered for about sixty years.
The Ontario Heritage Foundation bought the building in 1981; restoration ran 1987-1989 at a cost of about $29 million.
The complex was designated a National Historic Site of Canada in 1982 and is the last surviving Edwardian stacked theatres in the world.
w: The upper hall of the Elgin and Winter Garden -- the last Edwardian double-decker theatre left standing anywhere on earth. Thomas Lamb stacked the pair in 1913; the Winter Garden opened as a fantasy 'garden under the stars', hung with beech leaves and lanterns. Sound film shut it in 1928 and it sat dark for sixty years before its rescue.

hook: Begun in 1808, the oldest surviving lighthouse on the Great Lakes and one of Toronto's oldest buildings, tied to the city's most enduring ghost story.
facts: Construction began in 1808, making it the oldest existing lighthouse on the Great Lakes and one of Toronto's oldest structures, on the Toronto Islands.
Built to a height of about 52 feet (16 m), it was raised to about 82 feet (25 m) in 1832.
Its first keeper, German-born John Paul Radelmuller, was killed in January 1815; recent historical research confirmed his death and identified soldiers charged with (and acquitted of) the crime.
In 1893 keeper George Durnan reported finding part of a jawbone and coffin fragments near the lighthouse, though they could not be definitively linked to Radelmuller.
legend: Legend says soldiers from Fort York visited Radelmuller for his bootleg beer on 2 January 1815, a drunken dispute turned deadly, and the killers dismembered and hid the body. The lighthouse is said to be haunted by his ghost, the basis of Toronto's most famous ghost story. The haunting is folklore.
w: Begun in 1808, the oldest surviving lighthouse on the Great Lakes and one of Toronto's oldest buildings. Its first keeper, John Paul Radelmuller, was killed in January 1815. The legend runs that soldiers came for his bootleg beer, a quarrel turned deadly, and they hid the dismembered body -- and that his ghost walks still.

hook: A Gothic 1868 Jarvis Street mansion once home to the Massey family, now a Keg steakhouse trailing a much-told ghost story.
facts: Built in 1868 at 515 Jarvis Street for Arthur McMaster, nephew of senator and banker William McMaster, the Gothic Revival mansion was originally named Euclid Hall.
Hart Massey, the agricultural-machinery magnate, bought the house in 1882 and added features including a turret, verandah and greenhouse.
The building served as the first home of radio station CFRB in the 1920s and later housed an art gallery and the restaurant Julie's Mansion.
It became a Keg restaurant location in 1976 and remains one today, when it took the name Keg Mansion.
legend: The mansion is widely reputed to be haunted, most famously by the ghost of a maid said to have hanged herself in the foyer after the death of Lillian Massey, along with other reported apparitions. These accounts are folklore, popularised by ghost tours and Halloween features.
w: A Gothic 1868 mansion on Jarvis Street, built for a McMaster and bought in 1882 by the farm-machinery magnate Hart Massey, who added the turret and greenhouse. It has been a radio station, a gallery, and since 1976 a Keg steakhouse. The story diners pass around: a maid who hanged herself in the foyer, still seen on the stairs.

hook: A Victorian non-denominational cemetery in Cabbagetown holding the graves of Toronto's first mayor and a Father of Confederation.
facts: Opened in the 1850s at 200 Winchester Street to replace the Strangers' Burying Ground (established 1826, closed 1855), on the west side of the Don River valley north of Riverdale Farm.
It is a non-denominational cemetery, designed to accommodate people of all faiths, and is run by the non-profit Mount Pleasant Group of Cemeteries.
William Lyon Mackenzie, Toronto's first mayor and leader of the 1837 Upper Canada Rebellion, is buried here.
Other notable burials include George Brown, a Father of Confederation and founder of the Globe; world-champion oarsman Ned Hanlan; and NDP leader Jack Layton.
The grounds include 34 Commonwealth war graves from the two world wars.
w: A non-denominational Victorian cemetery in Cabbagetown, opened in the 1850s to take all faiths the older grounds turned away. Under its trees lie Toronto's first mayor, William Lyon Mackenzie, who led the 1837 rebellion; George Brown, a Father of Confederation; the oarsman Ned Hanlan; and Jack Layton.

hook: Toronto's oldest surviving building from the Town of York, a 1822 Georgian mansion physically rolled across downtown to escape demolition.
facts: A 2.5-storey late-Georgian house built in 1822 for Sir William Campbell, Chief Justice of Upper Canada, now a museum at 160 Queen Street West at University Avenue.
It is regarded as Toronto's oldest surviving building from the original Town of York.
In 1972 the entire house was moved from its original Adelaide Street site to its present location to save it from demolition.
The grounds host a permanent open-air exhibition, Lost & Found, displaying handcrafted carved stone fragments salvaged from demolished Toronto landmarks.

The Lost & Found stones come from buildings such as the old Toronto Star Building, the Bank of Toronto and Bank of Montreal headquarters, and the Imperial Oil Building, many originally rescued by Rosa and Spencer Clark of Guild Park.
w: Toronto's oldest building from the original Town of York -- a Georgian mansion of 1822 built for a chief justice. When demolition loomed in 1972, the whole house was jacked up and rolled across downtown to this corner. In the garden, the Lost & Found exhibit shows carved stone salvaged from vanished landmarks.

hook: A hidden two-level cloud-forest greenhouse with its own waterfall, wedged between bank towers off Temperance Street.
facts: Located at 14 Temperance Street, on a 0.6-acre (2,400 sq m) parcel granted to the city in the 1980s as part of a downtown development agreement.
Contains a small two-level greenhouse kept at the cool, moist conditions of a cloud rainforest, plus a multi-storey concrete waterfall with climbing walkways.
The surrounding park includes a monument to Toronto's construction workers depicting the different building trades.
Its landscape design won Baird Sampson Architects a Governor General's Medal in Architecture.
The conservatory closed in November 2018 for waterproofing repairs, with reopening estimated for late 2026.
w: A two-level cloud-forest greenhouse with its own concrete waterfall, tucked between bank towers off Temperance Street -- a cool, dripping rainforest a step from the Financial District. The little park around it carries a monument to the building trades. Closed for waterproofing since 2018, with a reopening pencilled for 2026.

hook: Toronto's smallest house, a roughly 300-square-foot bungalow built on a strip of land meant to be a laneway.
facts: Stands at 128 Day Avenue in the St. Clair West / Corso Italia area; OpenStreetMap labels the building 'Little House'.
Built in 1912 by contractor Arthur Weeden on a lot just about 7.5 feet wide and roughly 113 feet deep.
Around 300 square feet, it contains a bedroom, kitchen, living room and a narrow full bathroom.
Weeden reportedly said the strip was originally intended as a laneway, but the city never cut a curb, so he built on it to use the land.
The house was featured on The Ellen DeGeneres Show in January 2008.
w: Toronto's smallest house -- about 300 square feet on a strip of land only seven and a half feet wide, meant to be a laneway. When the city never cut the curb, contractor Arthur Weeden simply built a house on it in 1912. Bedroom, kitchen, living room and a sliver of a bathroom, all complete. A full home, on the width of a hallway.

hook: The surviving half of a Victorian semi-detached pair, sliced down its shared wall when its twin was demolished.
facts: Located at 54 1/2 St. Patrick Street, a short walk from St. Patrick subway station.
Built in the early 1890s as one of six identical bay-and-gable row houses on what was then called Dummer Street.
Developers acquired the rest of the row but the owners of 54 1/2 refused to sell, so workers cut the building in two along the property line, leaving its neighbour 54 demolished.
The house shared a load-bearing wall with number 54, making the separation a delicate piece of deconstruction.
It still stands and remains a private residence.
w: The surviving half of a Victorian row, sliced clean down its shared wall when the rest came down. Six identical bay-and-gable houses went up here in the 1890s; developers bought five, but the owners of 54 1/2 St Patrick refused to sell -- so the wreckers cut the building in two, leaving this half.

hook: A cluster of tilted green cubes balanced on a tower, Toronto's homage to Rotterdam's Dutch cube houses.
facts: Stands at 1 Sumach Street in Corktown; OpenStreetMap labels the building 'Cube House'.
Built in 1996 to a design by Ben Kutner and Jeff Brown, inspired by Dutch architect Piet Blom's cube houses in Rotterdam and Helmond.
The structure consists of tilted green geometric cubes set diagonally atop a supporting tower.
The property was designated under Ontario's heritage rules on February 4, 2026.
As of mid-2026 it still stands, but Block Developments has proposed replacing it with two towers of 55 and 59 storeys, and reclaimed material is slated to be reused in a commemorative artwork.
w: A cluster of tilted green cubes balanced on a tower in Corktown -- Toronto's homage to the upended cube houses of Rotterdam. Kutner and Brown built it in 1996, diagonals and all. Heritage-listed in 2026, yet a pair of 55- and 59-storey towers is proposed to replace it; salvaged pieces would go into a commemorative artwork.

hook: A split concrete sphere you can walk inside, sitting in a pool that spills toward Lake Ontario.
facts: Located in Harbour Square Park near the foot of Bay Street, just south of Queens Quay.
Created by artists John Fung and Paul Figueiredo and installed in 1995.
The hollow concrete sphere is bisected so a walkway leads into its interior.
It sits in a pool of water that feeds a small artificial waterfall toward the lake.
The sculpture underwent a concrete restoration to repair years of waterfront weathering.
w: A split concrete sphere you can walk inside, set in a pool that spills toward Lake Ontario at the foot of Bay Street. Fung and Figueiredo bisected it in 1995 so a walkway leads into the hollow heart; the water feeds a small waterfall behind. Not a sundial at all, despite the name. A quiet riddle on the waterfront.

hook: A black-and-white 'tabletop' floating eight storeys up on a dozen brightly coloured stilts above OCAD University.
facts: Located at 100 McCaul Street as part of OCAD University, beside Grange Park.
Designed by British architect Will Alsop with Robbie/Young + Wright Architects and completed in spring 2004.
The pixel-patterned rectangular 'table top' box houses the Faculty of Design and is raised about eight storeys above the existing campus buildings.
It is supported on twelve sloping, multicoloured steel legs that let the box float above the ground.
In 2004 it won a Royal Institute of British Architects (RIBA) Worldwide Award, praised as 'courageous, bold and just a little insane'.
w: A black-and-white 'tabletop' floating eight storeys up on a dozen brightly coloured stilts above OCAD. Will Alsop finished the pixel-patterned box in 2004, raising the design faculty over the campus on slanting steel legs. One award jury called it 'courageous, bold and just a little insane'. A building that decided to levitate.

hook: A two-tier fountain ringed by 27 cast-iron dogs spouting water up at a single golden bone.
facts: Located in Berczy Park, a triangular park bounded by Scott, Front and Wellington Streets in the St. Lawrence neighbourhood.
Designed by Montreal landscape architect Claude Cormier as part of a roughly $7.2-million park renovation; the park reopened on June 28, 2017.
The fountain features 27 dog sculptures, each spraying water from its mouth, all gazing up at a gilded bone at the top.
One lone cat, nicknamed 'Pam McCat', is included in honour of late councillor Pam McConnell, with a second cat perched on an electrical box at the park's southwest corner.
A ground-level trough provides drinking water for real dogs visiting the park.
w: A two-tier fountain ringed by 27 cast-iron dogs, every breed spouting water up at a single gilded bone. Claude Cormier designed it for the park's 2017 revamp; look close for the lone cat, 'Pam McCat', and a real dogs' drinking trough at the base. Pure deadpan whimsy. Twenty-seven hounds, one bone, no winner.

hook: A clifftop sculpture garden of salvaged building fragments arranged like classical ruins above the Scarborough Bluffs.
facts: Located at 201 Guildwood Parkway on the Scarborough Bluffs.
Rosa and Spencer Clark began collecting reliefs, columns and carvings from Toronto buildings as they were demolished, starting in the 1950s.

The open-air Greek Theatre was built from eight marble columns plus Corinthian capitals and arches salvaged from the demolished Bank of Toronto building (1912-1966); it staged its first performance in 1984.
The collection includes architectural remnants from over 60 buildings in Toronto and elsewhere in Ontario.
The Clarks ran the site as the artists' colony 'The Guild of All Arts' from 1932; it became a public park in 1978 when Metropolitan Toronto and the Province acquired it.
w: A clifftop garden of salvaged building fragments above the Scarborough Bluffs, arranged like classical ruins. From the 1950s Rosa and Spencer Clark rescued columns, reliefs and carvings as Toronto demolished its old buildings -- pieces of over 60 of them. Eight marble columns from the lost Bank of Toronto form an open-air Greek Theatre.

hook: An accidental wilderness on a man-made peninsula of construction rubble, now one of Toronto's richest bird sanctuaries.
facts: The spit is a man-made landfill peninsula begun in the 1950s as a Toronto Harbour Commission breakwater/outer-harbour project that was never needed for shipping.
It was built up from excavation waste (subway and tower spoil) plus dredged material from the Keating Channel.
More than 300 bird species have been recorded and around 45 breed there, including large ring-billed gull, cormorant and tern colonies.
It is designated an Important Bird Area by Nature Canada and Birds Canada.

The northern half, named Tommy Thompson Park after a former Toronto parks commissioner, is managed by the Toronto and Region Conservation Authority for conservation and recreation; the park is open to the public on weekends and holidays.
Public address is 1 Leslie Street (entrance at Leslie Street and Unwin Avenue).
w: An accidental wilderness on a man-made peninsula of rubble -- subway spoil, demolished towers and dredged muck, piled into the lake for a harbour never needed. Nature took it instead: over 300 bird species recorded, great colonies of gulls, cormorants and terns nesting on the wreckage. Open weekends to walkers and cyclists.

hook: A compact cedar hedge maze on Centre Island, a Centennial gift that was rebuilt from scratch after decades of neglect.
facts: The original maze was a 1967 gift from Toronto's Dutch-Canadian community for Canada's Centennial, designed by landscape designer Peter Vanderwerf.
The overgrown original cedars were removed in 2011 after years of neglect.
Mississauga businessman William Meany donated over $200,000 to rebuild it after revisiting the island in 2012.
Reconstruction ran from May to September 2014, planting more than 1,200 black cedars roughly 50 metres northwest of the original site.
It is a free, year-round attraction a few minutes' walk from the Centre Island pier.
w: A compact cedar hedge maze on Centre Island, first given by the city's Dutch-Canadian community for Canada's 1967 Centennial. Left to grow wild, it was torn out in 2011 -- then a businessman who'd loved it as a visitor gave over 200,000 dollars to rebuild it, 1,200 new cedars planted in 2014. Free to get lost in, year-round.

n: Trinity Square Labyrinth & Church of the Holy Trinity
a: Downtown Yonge, behind the Eaton Centre
hook: A Gothic Revival church and an outdoor walking labyrinth hidden in a pocket square wedged behind the Eaton Centre.
facts: The Church of the Holy Trinity is an Anglican Gothic Revival church built in 1847, funded by an anonymous donation from Mary Lambert Swale of Settle, England.
When the Eaton Centre was planned in the 1970s, parishioners successfully resisted demolition and forced the mall's design to be changed to preserve the church.
The square's Toronto Public Labyrinth is an eleven-circuit design modelled on the 13th-century labyrinth at Chartres Cathedral; as a labyrinth it has a single path and no dead ends.
The labyrinth lies on the former buried course of Taddle Creek.
Trinity Square is bounded by the Eaton Centre to the east and the Bell Trinity Square complex to the south and west.
w: A Gothic Revival church of 1847 and an outdoor walking labyrinth, hidden in a pocket square behind the Eaton Centre. When the mall was planned, the parish forced the design to bend around the church. The labyrinth copies the one at Chartres -- a single winding path, no dead ends -- laid over the buried course of Taddle Creek.

hook: A rainbow painted across a railway underpass in the Don Valley as a teenager's memorial, repeatedly buffed by the city and repainted in defiance.
facts: The rainbow was first painted in 1971-72 by B.C. (Berg) Johnson, a Norwegian teenager, on the CN rail tunnel on the east side of the Don Valley Parkway south of Lawrence Avenue East.
Johnson painted it in memory of a friend who died in a crash on the DVP; he said she had called rainbows 'smiles from heaven.'
The city repeatedly painted over it with grey, and Johnson repeatedly returned to repaint it.
Care of the mural was handed to Mural Routes; a restoration led by Rob Matejka and Anthony DeLacruz began on 9 October 2012.
In summer 2013 local youth from Flemingdon Neighbourhood Services added interior artwork of people in changing seasons.
It is reached from Moccasin Trail Park via the East Don Trail, about a five-minute walk from the parking lot.
legend: Local lore holds that the tunnel is haunted by the spirit of the young woman the rainbow memorialises.
w: A rainbow painted across a Don Valley railway underpass in 1972 by B.C. Johnson, a Norwegian teenager, for a friend killed on the parkway -- she had called rainbows 'smiles from heaven'. The city kept greying it out; he kept coming back to repaint it. Mural Routes took it on and restored it in 2012.

hook: A glowing steel fence along Eastern Avenue that tells the area's story from the ice age to the Underground Railroad family who once lived on the spot.
facts: The fence runs along Eastern Avenue at Sumach Street, beside Inglenook Community School (the former Sackville Street School, 19 Sackville St).
It was installed in June 2015 by Toronto artist Scott Eunson and collaborator Marianne Lovink, working with Inglenook students.
Made of corten and stainless steel with LED lighting behind it to suggest fire at night, it depicts local history from the ice age to the present.

The site was the home of Thornton and Lucie Blackburn, escaped slaves who fled Kentucky via the Underground Railroad and settled in Toronto, from 1834 to 1890; their house site is a National Historic Site of Canada.
Thornton Blackburn founded Toronto's first horse-drawn cab company in 1834.
The artwork incorporates household items (silver spoons, lace) recovered during archaeological excavation of the Blackburn home site.
w: A steel fence on Eastern Avenue that glows at night and tells the area's story from the ice age to the Underground Railroad. It marks the home of Thornton and Lucie Blackburn, who fled slavery in Kentucky -- where Thornton founded Toronto's first cab company in 1834. Eunson and students set it with spoons and lace from the dig.

hook: A towering shark mural on a Yorkville back wall honouring the Toronto filmmaker and shark-conservation activist Rob Stewart.
facts: The mural is on the rear wall of Opus Restaurant at 37 Prince Arthur Avenue in Yorkville.
It was painted by Toronto-based artists birdO and GETSO over 1-8 September 2017 and unveiled on 9 September 2017.
It is part of PangeaSeed Foundation's Dakuwaqa Project, which dedicated murals to Rob Stewart in Toronto, Miami, Los Angeles, New Zealand and Australia.
Rob Stewart, a Toronto native, made the documentary 'Sharkwater' and died in a diving accident in January 2017.
The mural depicts a portal into the ocean, spotlighting overfishing, marine plastic pollution and biodiversity loss.
w: A towering shark surges across a Yorkville back wall, a tribute to the Toronto filmmaker Rob Stewart, who made 'Sharkwater' and died diving in 2017. Painted by birdO and GETSO in 2017 for PangeaSeed's worldwide series of Stewart murals, it opens like a portal into the ocean -- a plea against overfishing and the plastic tide.

hook: A small semicircular concrete deck at the end of a quiet residential street with a clean sightline to the CN Tower across the Don Valley.
facts: The lookout sits at the western end of Chester Hill Road, north of Broadview and Danforth Avenues, about a ten-minute walk from Broadview subway station.
It overlooks the Don Valley and the Prince Edward Viaduct with the downtown skyline and CN Tower beyond.
The deck is painted with a large blue-and-gold geometric 'astrology wheel' by Toronto sidewalk artist @whatsvictorupto.
The land from the lookout down to the Don Valley Parkway is owned by the TRCA and leased by the City of Toronto.
Local residents run annual spring clean-ups of the site.
w: A small concrete deck at the dead end of a quiet residential street, with a clean sightline across the Don Valley to the CN Tower. The platform is painted with a big blue-and-gold 'astrology wheel' by a local sidewalk artist; neighbours run the spring clean-up. A locals' viewpoint, hiding in plain sight above the parkway.
